does: This paper adapts Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) to address hallucination in large multimodal models by training vision-language models to align textual outputs with visual information. The core method involves human annotators comparing pairs of model responses to identify more hallucinated outputs, enabling reward signal extraction for RL training. The authors introduce Factually Augmented RLHF, which enriches the reward model with auxiliary factual information including image captions and ground-truth multiple-choice options. This augmentation reduces reward hacking and improves alignment performance. Additionally, they enhance GPT-4-generated instruction-tuning data by incorporating human-written image-text pairs to strengthen general model capabilities, creating a more robust training signal that better grounds language generation in multimodal context.
connects: This work builds directly on RLHF techniques that have proven successful in aligning large language models with human preferences, extending them to the multimodal domain where vision-language alignment is crucial. It responds to well-documented hallucination problems in LMMs where models generate plausible but visually unsupported text, a limitation that emerged as these models scaled across modalities. The approach connects to prior work on vision-language understanding and instruction tuning for multimodal systems, while incorporating insights from reward modeling and the reward hacking problem identified in recent RLHF studies. The use of factual augmentation relates to earlier work on improving reward model robustness and addressing distributional shift between training and deployment in reinforcement learning contexts.
enables: This framework enables more reliable multimodal AI systems by providing a systematic method to enforce grounding between vision and language outputs at scale. The factually augmented reward model design could inspire similar approaches to robustness in other multimodal tasks beyond hallucination, such as temporal grounding in video or cross-modal reasoning. The methodology opens pathways for developing better evaluation protocols for multimodal alignment that move beyond traditional captioning metrics. Future work could extend this to other modality combinations, integrate additional factual sources dynamically, or develop weakly-supervised variants that reduce annotation costs. This approach also points toward more principled methods for detecting and correcting misalignment failures in deployed multimodal systems, with applications to long-form multimodal reasoning and embodied AI tasks.
